draft.introduction: Vitruvius’s De Architectura (c. 30 BCE) is the only surviving ancient treatise on architecture, covering everything from temple design to water supply. Its three principles—firmitas, utilitas, venustas (strength, utility, beauty)—defined architectural theory through the Renaissance.

translation.text: The architect should be equipped with knowledge of many branches of study and varied kinds of learning, for his judgment is called upon in all the arts. This knowledge arises from practice and theory. Practice is the continuous exercise of the hands. Theory is the ability to demonstrate and explain the things wrought by proportional reasoning.
translation.original_text: Architecti est scientia pluribus disciplinis et variis eruditionibus ornata

translator_notes.note: Vitruvius’s insistence that the architect must master geometry, philosophy, music, medicine, and law makes architecture the quintessential interdisciplinary art. His emphasis on proportion derived from the human body inspired Leonardo’s Vitruvian Man.
